reviewer: solo new-family build 2026-07-21: code-language identification proven FIRST in headless Chrome (transformers.js text-classification WASM q8), then the deployed page driven end-to-end. Idiomatic snippets classify to the right language at ~1.00. No media — all input is text/code; samples are first-party idiomatic snippets.

rubric.evidence: Correctness verified in headless Chrome (transformers.js text-classification, WASM q8) BEFORE building. Idiomatic snippets classify to the right language: Python 1.00, JavaScript 1.00, Java 1.00, Go 1.00, Ruby 1.00, PHP 0.99. Deployed page (scripts/validate-code-language-id.mjs 11/11) reproduces this — the pre-filled Python auto-classifies and each sample chip (Go/Ruby/PHP/Java) is named correctly. The verdict + bars are the model's real softmax over the 6 languages, not canned.
rubric.notes: DISTINCT from the built code models: codebert-fill-mask predicts masked tokens, code-embeddings does retrieval, starcoder-fim completes code — this classifies WHICH LANGUAGE the code is.

rubric.evidence: Input a code snippet (text) → softmax over 6 languages (go, java, javascript, php, python, ruby). A distinct capability + input from the built code models and the built text classifiers (sentiment/emotion/etc.): it reads SOURCE CODE and returns a language.
rubric.notes: top_k:6 returns the whole distribution so the confidence chart is complete.

rubric.evidence: At-a-glance declares the model (onnx-community/CodeBERTa-language-id-ONNX, linked, ONNX build of huggingface/CodeBERTa-language-id), task (text-classification), the 6 languages, architecture (CodeBERTa / RoBERTa-on-code), quantisation (q8), backend (WASM), download (~84 MB) and license (Apache-2.0). The How-it-works block shows the real pipeline call. No invented API.
rubric.notes: transformers.js is loaded via the shared lib/webai.js loadPipeline; the 6 labels come from the model config.

rubric.evidence: Loads through the shared createModelLoader auto-init architecture (model {modelId:'onnx-community/CodeBERTa-language-id-ONNX', runtime:'transformers.js', dtype:'q8', sizeMB:84}). First visit shows an explicit Download (~84 MB); the ONNX + tokenizer are cached by transformers.js so a returning visit auto-initialises (checking -> ready) with NO Download click. Verified: fresh profile showed Download; clicking it enabled the editor.
rubric.notes: The never-silently-download guarantee + auto-init on return hold via the shared loader; inference is off the main thread in a module Worker.

rubric.evidence: checking -> Download (~84 MB) -> ready (editor enabled, pre-filled sample auto-classified) -> live re-classification debounced 300 ms after typing. Classification failures surface on the page in a role=status .err region, not only the console. Verified download -> ready -> classify end-to-end.

A real editable code textarea (type/paste, monospace, spellcheck off) plus six language sample chips that load an idiomatic snippet and re-classify. Every control is labelled and keyboard-operable; no dead controls.
rubric.notes: The chips are a quick way to see each language without pasting your own code.

rubric.evidence: The verdict + all 6 bars are the model's real softmax; the readout shows the live backend + latency. The page is HONEST about limits: it reads syntax, so a very short/ambiguous snippet can be misread (a bare Go one-liner may score as JavaScript), the bars make a not-sure spread visible, and it only knows the 6 CodeSearchNet languages — it will always pick the nearest for a language it doesn't know.
rubric.notes: Samples are first-party idiomatic snippets whose classifications were verified, not asserted.

rubric.evidence: The code textarea has a visible label; the verdict is role=status aria-live; each language bar shows its name as text (colour is decorative); every chip has an accessible name. The output region updates are announced.
rubric.notes: The confidence bars are readable as text (language + %) with no reliance on colour.

rubric.evidence: The textarea, chips and bars all use fluid widths; the code textarea scrolls horizontally in its own box (white-space:pre) so long lines never break the page; the bar grid stays legible. Verified 0 horizontal overflow at desktop AND mobile 360px (validate 11/11), 0 console errors.
rubric.notes: overflow-x:auto on the code box keeps wide code contained.

rubric.evidence: Inference runs in a dedicated module Worker; a CodeBERTa forward pass on a short snippet is fast on WASM, and classification is debounced 300 ms so typing stays smooth. The ~84 MB download is one-time (cached).
rubric.notes: The whole 6-language distribution comes from a single forward pass.

guidanceConsulted.query: classify text in a worker and keep typing responsive (INP), debounce updates
guidanceConsulted.recommendation: Move inference off the main thread; debounce rapid updates so INP stays low.
guidanceConsulted.appliedOrException: applied — classification runs in a module Worker; live re-classification is debounced 300 ms.
guidanceConsulted.evidence: typing stays smooth; the editor never blocks on a forward pass

guidanceConsulted.query: highlight arbitrary code / detect code language for syntax highlighting
guidanceConsulted.recommendation: Detecting a snippet's language is the prerequisite for choosing a grammar; keep the detection client-side.
guidanceConsulted.appliedOrException: applied (context) — this demo IS the language-detection step; it runs entirely client-side so an editor could pick a highlighting grammar without sending the code anywhere.
guidanceConsulted.evidence: language named on-device from the snippet alone

guidanceConsulted.query: accessible live results that don't rely on colour
guidanceConsulted.recommendation: Announce dynamic results in an aria-live region and never encode meaning in colour alone.
guidanceConsulted.appliedOrException: applied — the verdict is role=status aria-live; each language bar shows its name as text with colour purely decorative.
guidanceConsulted.evidence: results readable as text; colour is redundant

openQuestions: Would a confidence threshold ('not sure — could be X or Y') help when two look-alike languages (e.g. JavaScript vs TypeScript-looking JS) are close?
Is there a cleanly-licensed model covering more of today's common languages (TypeScript, Rust, C#, C++, SQL) to widen the six CodeSearchNet classes?
followUpGoals: Chain the detected language into a client-side syntax highlighter for a live-highlight demo.
Add an 'uncertain' banner when the top-1 is low or the top-2 are close.
Explore a broader-language model (or an ensemble) beyond the six CodeSearchNet languages.
summary: Code language ID (CodeBERTa, 6 languages) — a distinct text family: name a snippet's programming language (Go/Java/JavaScript/PHP/Python/Ruby) from syntax alone, vs the built code models that mask/retrieve/complete code. Proven correct in the browser on transformers.js WASM q8 (all six idiomatic samples classified right). ~84 MB, Apache-2.0, no media. A debounced code editor + sample chips + a 6-language confidence chart; index demo complete + verified 11/11. Honest that short/ambiguous snippets can be misread and that it only knows the six CodeSearchNet languages. A live syntax-highlight chain, an 'uncertain' banner, and a broader-language model are recorded follow-ups.
